<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Cohesion of 0.87 for a 12-file mixed source+test zone is strong; the subsystem boundary is well-drawn around the task usage collection and lifecycle management concern.
[observation] [warning] sourcevision (domain layer) imports this web-server zone once; domain packages should not depend on web-tier modules — verify this reference is a type-only import from shared-types.ts that can be relocated to a foundation-tier package if it grows.
[observation] [info] task-usage.ts classified as a gateway correctly centralizes upstream data access and prevents direct dependencies from proliferating across the scheduler files — consistent with the project's gateway conventions.
[relationship] [critical] sourcevision (domain tier) has 1 import targeting the web-server zone that likely resolves to web-task-usage-scheduler/shared-types.ts; this inverts the four-tier dependency hierarchy at a concrete file level — the referenced type must be relocated to a foundation-tier package (@n-dx/llm-client or a new shared package) to restore tier integrity.
[suggestion] [info] shared-types.ts carries a @public stability marker and is validated by a dedicated conformance test, indicating it is a cross-zone interface contract, not an incidental types file. The zone name 'scheduler' obscures this contract role. Consider renaming the file to task-usage-contract.ts or task-usage-interface.ts and adding a comment in the zone's README (or test file header) noting that changes require coordinated updates across consuming zones.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
- Strong cohesion (0.87) across 12 files (source + tests) confirms this is a genuine, well-bounded subsystem centered on the task usage lifecycle — collection, incremental update, and scheduled expiry.
- The task-usage.ts gateway correctly centralizes all upstream data access into one module, keeping the scheduler's external dependencies explicit and auditable in line with the declared gateway pattern.
- One inbound import from sourcevision (domain layer) into this web-tier zone warrants investigation — domain packages should not depend on web-server modules; this may be a shared-types reference that belongs in a lower-tier foundation module.
- Cohesion of 0.87 for a 12-file mixed source+test zone is strong; the subsystem boundary is well-drawn around the task usage collection and lifecycle management concern.
- task-usage.ts classified as a gateway correctly centralizes upstream data access and prevents direct dependencies from proliferating across the scheduler files — consistent with the project's gateway conventions.
- sourcevision (domain layer) imports this web-server zone once; domain packages should not depend on web-tier modules — verify this reference is a type-only import from shared-types.ts that can be relocated to a foundation-tier package if it grows.
- The sourcevision→web-server cross-zone import (1 edge) directly targets this zone rather than web-application-core; if shared-types.ts contains a type used by sourcevision, that type is in the wrong tier — it should live in @n-dx/llm-client or a new shared-types foundation package.
- sourcevision (domain tier) has 1 import targeting the web-server zone that likely resolves to web-task-usage-scheduler/shared-types.ts; this inverts the four-tier dependency hierarchy at a concrete file level — the referenced type must be relocated to a foundation-tier package (@n-dx/llm-client or a new shared package) to restore tier integrity.
- shared-types.ts is a pure-types file (no runtime logic) but has a dedicated test file (shared-types.test.ts) that validates structural conformance, JSON round-trips, and cross-zone callback signatures — the test file reflects the file's role as a cross-zone interface contract rather than a simple type alias collection. The zone name 'scheduler' does not communicate that it owns a stable, explicitly-tested cross-zone contract surface, which may lead contributors to treat shared-types.ts as a low-risk refactoring target when it actually requires coordinated updates across all consuming modules.
- shared-types.ts carries a @public stability marker and is validated by a dedicated conformance test, indicating it is a cross-zone interface contract, not an incidental types file. The zone name 'scheduler' obscures this contract role. Consider renaming the file to task-usage-contract.ts or task-usage-interface.ts and adding a comment in the zone's README (or test file header) noting that changes require coordinated updates across consuming zones.
